# anti-default-output

## дёҖеҸҘиҜқжҖ»з»“

`anti-default-output` жҳҜдёҖдёӘжҠҠвҖңе…ҲиҜҶеҲ«жЁЎеһӢй»ҳи®Өз”ҹжҲҗи·Ҝеҫ„пјҢеҶҚжңүж„ҸиҜҶеҒҸзҰ»е®ғвҖқе°ҒиЈ…жҲҗ skill зҡ„е°ҸеһӢд»“еә“пјҢйҮҚзӮ№дёҚжҳҜз»ҷдёҖдёӘеӣәе®ҡжЁЎжқҝпјҢиҖҢжҳҜе»әз«ӢдёҖеҘ—иҫ“еҮәзә еҒҸзәӘеҫӢгҖӮ

## ж‘ҳиҰҒ

- иҝҷжҳҜйЎ№зӣ®д»“еә“зҡ„дё»иҜҙжҳҺжқҘжәҗпјҢREADME е’Ң `SKILL.md` е…ұеҗҢе®ҡд№үдәҶиҝҷдёӘйЎ№зӣ®еҲ°еә•еңЁи§ЈеҶід»Җд№Ҳй—®йўҳгҖӮ
- д»“еә“жҠҠй—®йўҳи®Іеҫ—еҫҲзӣҙжҺҘпјҡжЁЎеһӢеӨ©з„¶дјҡж»‘еҗ‘й«ҳжҰӮзҺҮгҖҒжңҖе№іеқҮгҖҒжңҖе®үе…Ёзҡ„иҫ“еҮәпјҢиҖҢиҝҷдәӣиҫ“еҮәиҷҪз„¶дёҚдёҖе®ҡй”ҷпјҢдҪҶз»Ҹеёёж— иҒҠгҖҒдҝқе®ҲгҖҒеёҰжҳҺжҳҫжЁЎжқҝж„ҹгҖӮ
- иҝҷдёӘ skill зҡ„ж ёеҝғжңәеҲ¶дёҚжҳҜвҖңи§„е®ҡдҪ еҝ…йЎ»жҖҺд№ҲеҶҷвҖқпјҢиҖҢжҳҜиҰҒжұӮжЁЎеһӢеңЁз”ҹжҲҗеүҚе…ҲиҮӘжЈҖпјҡиҮӘе·ұжҳҜдёҚжҳҜеҸҲеңЁиө°й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„гҖӮ
- еҰӮжһңеҲӨж–ӯзӯ”жЎҲжҳҜвҖңжҳҜвҖқпјҢе°ұдёҚиҰҒзӣҙжҺҘдәӨеҮәз¬¬дёҖзүҲпјҢиҖҢиҰҒдё»еҠЁжҚўдёҖдёӘжӣҙжңүи§’еәҰгҖҒжӣҙжңүеҲӨж–ӯгҖҒжӣҙдёҚйӮЈд№ҲеғҸеҘ—и·ҜжЁЎжқҝзҡ„ж–№еҗ‘гҖӮ
- йЎ№зӣ®з»“жһ„д№ҹеҫҲиҪ»пјҡ`SKILL.md` и®ІйҖҡз”Ёж–№жі•пјҢ`references/` иҙҹиҙЈеҲҶеңәжҷҜжІүж·Җеёёи§Ғй»ҳи®Өй”ҷиҜҜе’ҢеҒҸзҰ»ж–№еҗ‘пјӣеҪ“еүҚиҮіе°‘иҰҶзӣ–еүҚз«Ҝи®ҫи®Ўе’ҢдёҖиҲ¬еҶҷдҪңгҖӮ
- д»Һд»“еә“з»“жһ„зңӢпјҢе®ғжӣҙеғҸдёҖдёӘеҸҜжү©еұ•зҡ„вҖңзә еҒҸеһӢ skillвҖқж ·жң¬пјҢиҖҢдёҚеҸӘжҳҜдёҖж¬ЎжҖ§зҡ„ prompt зүҮж®өгҖӮ

## е…ій”®зӮ№

- е®ғжҠҠвҖңй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮәвҖқзҗҶи§ЈжҲҗжЁЎеһӢе·ҘдҪңжңәеҲ¶зҡ„иҮӘз„¶з»“жһңпјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜеҚ•зәҜжҠҠй—®йўҳеҪ’з»“дёәжЁЎеһӢеҒ·жҮ’гҖӮ
- е®ғж—ўеҸҚеҜ№ bland зҡ„е№іеқҮеҢ–иҫ“еҮәпјҢд№ҹеҸҚеҜ№иҝҮеәҰи°„еӘҡгҖҒж°ёиҝңдёӯеәёзҡ„еӣһзӯ”и·Ҝеҫ„гҖӮ
- е®ғзҡ„ж–№жі•и®әж ёеҝғжҳҜдёүжӯҘпјҡиҜҶеҲ«й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„гҖҒжҢҮеҮәдёҚиҜҘз»§з»ӯиө°зҡ„иҢғејҸгҖҒз»ҷеҮәеҸҜеҒҸзҰ»зҡ„ж–№еҗ‘гҖӮ
- `references/` зҡ„еӯҳеңЁеҫҲе…ій”®пјҢиҝҷж„Ҹе‘ізқҖе®ғеҸҜд»ҘжҢүеңәжҷҜжҢҒз»ӯжү©еұ•пјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜеҒңз•ҷеңЁдёҖеҸҘжҠҪиұЎеҸЈеҸ·гҖӮ

## ж–№жі•жӢҶи§Ј

- е®ғе…ҲжҠҠй—®йўҳеҜ№иұЎиҜҙжё…жҘҡпјҢд№ҹе°ұжҳҜ [[й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„]]пјҡйӮЈдёӘжңҖзЁіеҰҘгҖҒжңҖе№іеқҮгҖҒжңҖе®№жҳ“дёҚеҒҮжҖқзҙўе°ұдәӨеҮәжқҘзҡ„зүҲжң¬гҖӮ
- з„¶еҗҺиҰҒжұӮжЁЎеһӢеҒҡдёҖж¬Ў [[иҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖ]]пјҢеңЁжӯЈејҸз”ҹжҲҗеүҚе…Ҳй—®иҮӘе·ұпјҡиҝҷжҳҜдёҚжҳҜжүҖжңү AI йғҪдјҡз»ҷеҮәзҡ„зӯ”жЎҲгҖӮ
- жңҖеҗҺжүҚиҝӣе…Ҙ [[еҸҚй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮә]]пјҢд№ҹе°ұжҳҜжңүж„ҸиҜҶең°жҚўдёҖдёӘжӣҙе…·дҪ“гҖҒжӣҙжңүз«ӢеңәгҖҒжҲ–жӣҙдёҚеҘ—и·Ҝзҡ„ж–№еҗ‘гҖӮ
- иҝҷдёӘйЎәеәҸеҫҲйҮҚиҰҒпјҢеӣ дёәе®ғиҜҙжҳҺд»“еә“и®Ізҡ„дёҚжҳҜвҖңеҶҷеҫ—зү№еҲ«дёҖзӮ№вҖқпјҢиҖҢжҳҜвҖңе…ҲиҜҶеҲ«й»ҳи®ӨпјҢеҶҚеҶіе®ҡжҖҺд№ҲеҒҸвҖқгҖӮ

## иҜҒжҚ®жҲ–еҺҹж–Үзәҝзҙў

- README зӣҙжҺҘжҠҠй—®йўҳеҪ’зәідёәпјҡAI й»ҳи®ӨеҖҫеҗ‘жҰӮзҺҮжңҖеӨ§зҡ„иҫ“еҮәпјҢиҖҢжңҖе№іеқҮзҡ„иҫ“еҮәеҫҖеҫҖжңҖдҝқе®ҲгҖҒжңҖжІЎзү№иүІгҖӮ
- `SKILL.md` жҳҺзЎ®иҰҒжұӮжЁЎеһӢеңЁз”ҹжҲҗеүҚе…Ҳй—®иҮӘе·ұпјҡвҖңжҲ‘зҺ°еңЁиҰҒз»ҷеҮәзҡ„пјҢжҳҜдёҚжҳҜйӮЈдёӘдёҚеҒҮжҖқзҙўе°ұдјҡз»ҷеҮәзҡ„зүҲжң¬пјҹвҖқ
- `SKILL.md` иҝҳжҠҠй—®йўҳиҝӣдёҖжӯҘжӢҶжҲҗдёӨеұӮпјҡдёҖеұӮжҳҜй«ҳжҰӮзҺҮи·Ҝеҫ„пјҢдёҖеұӮжҳҜеҒҸеҘҪеҜ№йҪҗеёҰжқҘзҡ„и°„еӘҡеҖҫеҗ‘гҖӮ
- `references/frontend-design.md` е’Ң `references/writing.md` йғҪйҮҮз”ЁвҖңй»ҳи®Өй”ҷиҜҜиҜҶеҲ« + зҰҒжӯўйЎ№ + еҒҸзҰ»ж–№еҗ‘вҖқзҡ„з»“жһ„пјҢиҜҙжҳҺиҝҷдёӘд»“еә“зЎ®е®һеңЁжҠҠж–№жі•иҗҪеҲ°е…·дҪ“йўҶеҹҹгҖӮ

## еҸҜзӣҙжҺҘеӣһиҜҒзҡ„иғҪеҠӣжҲ–дё»еј 

- иҝҷдёӘйЎ№зӣ®зЎ®е®һжҸҗдҫӣдәҶдёҖеҘ—еҸҜеӨҚз”Ёзҡ„ skill з»“жһ„пјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜеҸӘжңүйӣ¶ж•ЈжҸҗзӨәиҜҚгҖӮ
- е®ғзЎ®е®һејәи°ғз”ҹжҲҗеүҚиҮӘжЈҖгҖҒиҙҹйқўзӨәдҫӢзәҰжқҹгҖҒеҲҶйўҶеҹҹ reference иҝҷдёүзұ»жңәеҲ¶гҖӮ
- еҪ“еүҚд»“еә“е·Із»ҸжҠҠеүҚз«Ҝи®ҫи®Ўе’ҢеҶҷдҪңеңәжҷҜиҗҪжҲҗдәҶеҸҜзӣҙжҺҘеӨҚз”Ёзҡ„еҸӮиҖғж–Үд»¶гҖӮ
- `SKILL.md` йҮҢзҡ„иҮӘжЈҖй—®йўҳе’ҢеҒҸзҰ»ж–№еҗ‘пјҢи¶ід»Ҙж”Ҝж’‘жҠҠвҖңй»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„вҖқе’ҢвҖңиҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖвҖқиҝӣдёҖжӯҘжҠҪжҲҗеҸҜеӨҚз”ЁиҠӮзӮ№гҖӮ

## жӣҙеҒҸйЎ№зӣ®ж–№иҮӘиҝ°жҲ–еҫ…йӘҢиҜҒзҡ„еҲӨж–ӯ

- вҖңиҝҷж ·еҒҡиғҪзЁіе®ҡдә§еҮәжӣҙй«ҳиҙЁйҮҸеҶ…е®№вҖқзӣ®еүҚжӣҙеғҸж–№жі•дё»еј пјҢиҝҳзјәжӣҙеӨҡеӨ–йғЁж ·жң¬ж”Ҝж’‘гҖӮ
- README йҮҢеҜ№вҖңAI е…¬еҸёи®ӯз»ғеҫ—и¶ҠжқҘи¶ҠдёҚиҜҙдәәиҜқвҖқиҝҷзұ»иЎЁиҝ°пјҢжӣҙйҖӮеҗҲдҪңдёәз«ӢеңәиғҢжҷҜпјҢдёҚйҖӮеҗҲзӣҙжҺҘжІүж·ҖжҲҗе®ўи§Ӯз»“и®әгҖӮ
- иҝҷдёӘж–№жі•и·ЁеӨҡе°‘еңәжҷҜйғҪеҗҢж ·жңүж•ҲпјҢеҪ“еүҚд№ҹиҝҳйңҖиҰҒжӣҙеӨҡжқҘжәҗжқҘйӘҢиҜҒгҖӮ

## жҲ‘зҡ„еҲӨж–ӯ

- иҝҷд»ҪжқҘжәҗи¶ід»Ҙж”Ҝж’‘дёҖдёӘзЁіе®ҡжҰӮеҝөйЎөпјҢдҪҶиҝҳдёҚеӨҹж”Ҝж’‘жӣҙй«ҳеұӮзҡ„дё»йўҳйЎөгҖӮ
- е®ғжңҖеҖјеҫ—жІүж·Җзҡ„дёҚжҳҜвҖңеҺ» AI е‘івҖқиҝҷз§ҚжіӣеҢ–иҜҙжі•пјҢиҖҢжҳҜвҖңе…ҲиҜҶеҲ«й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„пјҢеҶҚдё»еҠЁеҒҸзҰ»вҖқзҡ„ж–№жі•жң¬иә«гҖӮ
- зӣ®еүҚжңҖеҗҲйҖӮзҡ„жҺҘжі•пјҢжҳҜжҠҠе®ғдҪңдёә [[Skill]] зҡ„дёҖдёӘзә еҒҸеһӢж ·жң¬пјҢеҗҢж—¶жҠҠ [[еҸҚй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮә]]гҖҒ[[й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„]]гҖҒ[[иҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖ]] иҝҷеҮ дёӘж–№жі•иҠӮзӮ№жҳҫејҸжӢҶејҖгҖӮ

# Skill

## е®ҡд№ү

еңЁиҝҷеә§зҹҘиҜҶеә“йҮҢпјҢ`Skill` жҢҮзҡ„жҳҜ AI зі»з»ҹдёӯеҸҜжҢүйңҖи°ғз”Ёзҡ„иғҪеҠӣеұӮпјҢз”ЁжқҘжүҝиҪҪзү№е®ҡе·ҘдҪңжөҒгҖҒдёҠдёӢж–ҮжіЁе…Ҙж–№ејҸгҖҒе·Ҙе…·дҪҝз”ЁжЁЎејҸе’Ңд»»еҠЎеһӢжү§иЎҢз»ҸйӘҢгҖӮ

## дёәд»Җд№ҲйҮҚиҰҒ

еҰӮжһңжүҖжңүиғҪеҠӣйғҪеҸӘиғҪйқ еҚіж—¶ prompt зҺ°еңәжӢјжҺҘпјҢзі»з»ҹе°ұеҫҲйҡҫеҪўжҲҗй•ҝжңҹеӨҚз”ЁгҖӮSkill зҡ„ж„Ҹд№үпјҢжҳҜжҠҠвҖңдјҡеҒҡдёҖж¬ЎвҖқеҸҳжҲҗвҖңд»ҘеҗҺиҝҳиғҪзЁіе®ҡеҶҚеҒҡвҖқгҖӮ

зҙўеј•е’ҢиҝҗиЎҢйқўд№ҹеҗҢжӯҘжӣҙж–°дәҶпјҡ[жҰӮеҝөзҙўеј•.md](/Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/_index/%E6%A6%82%E5%BF%B5%E7%B4%A2%E5%BC%95.md)гҖҒ[жқҘжәҗзҙўеј•.md](/Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/_index/%E6%9D%A5%E6%BA%90%E7%B4%A2%E5%BC%95.md)гҖҒ[жҖ»и§Ҳ.md](/Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/_index/%E6%80%BB%E8%A7%88.md)гҖҒ[log.md](/Users/liuyifu/Documents/123/MyLLMWiki/_index/log.md)гҖӮжҲ‘иҝҳйЎәжүӢеҒҡдәҶиҮӘжЈҖпјҢеҪ“еүҚи®Ўж•°жҳҜ `sources=7 / concepts=7 / topics=2 / relations=0`пјҢй“ҫжҺҘд№ҹйғҪжҺҘдёҠдәҶгҖӮиҝҷдёӘзӣ®еҪ•жң¬иә«дёҚжҳҜ git д»“еә“пјҢжүҖд»ҘжІЎжі•еҒҡ git зә§ж ЎйӘҢпјӣйҷӨжӯӨд№ӢеӨ–пјҢиҝҷиҪ®з»“жһ„дёҠжҳҜе№ІеҮҖзҡ„гҖӮ

# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

и®© `Skill` е’Ң [[й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„]]гҖҒ[[иҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖ]]гҖҒ[[еҸҚй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮә]] зҡ„е…ізі»дёҚеҸӘжҳҜвҖңзӣёе…івҖқпјҢиҝҳдҪ“зҺ°еҮәе®ғиғҪжүҝиҪҪиҝҷдәӣж–№жі•иҠӮзӮ№зҡ„з»„еҗҲгҖӮ

иҝҷж¬Ўе…ҲдёҚеҶҚж–°е»әйЎөпјҢдҝқжҢҒж”¶ж•ӣгҖӮ

еҰӮжһңдҪ зӮ№еӨҙпјҢжҲ‘е°ұзӣҙжҺҘж”№гҖӮ

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

жҜ”еҰӮеҸ«вҖңMethod-Layer Extraction RuleвҖқгҖӮ
жҳҺзЎ®иҜҙжҳҺпјҡ
жңүдәӣеҶ…е®№иҷҪз„¶иЎЁйқўжҳҜзҹӯеҸҘпјҢдҪҶеҰӮжһңе®ғзЁіе®ҡеӣһзӯ”дәҶвҖңеӨ„зҗҶд»Җд№ҲеҜ№иұЎгҖҒеҰӮдҪ•еҲӨж–ӯгҖҒеҰӮдҪ•жү§иЎҢвҖқпјҢе°ұеҸҜд»ҘжӢҶжҲҗеӨҡдёӘеҺҹеӯҗжҰӮеҝөпјҢжҲ–иҖ…иҮіе°‘еңЁжқҘжәҗйЎөйҮҢжҳҫејҸеҶҷеҮәж–№жі•й“ҫи·ҜгҖӮ
еҸҜз”Ё `й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„ -> иҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖ -> еҸҚй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮә` еҪ“дҫӢеӯҗгҖӮ

2. еңЁ `Concept vs Relation Rule` еҗҺйқўпјҢеҠ дёҖдёӘдё“й—Ёзҡ„е°Ҹи§„еҲҷгҖӮ

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

61:## Core Rules
76:## Default Execution Policy
86:5. extract or update concepts when the source clearly supports them
160:## Concept vs Relation Rule

232:### 1. Ingest workflow

- `references/page-contracts.md`
- `references/source-bundle-spec.md`
- `references/autonomy-policy.md`
- `references/relation-policy.md`

## Obsidian Companion Skills Awareness

When the task overlaps with Obsidian-native formats or tools, explicitly consider these companion skills from `kepano/obsidian-skills` if they are available in the environment:

- `obsidian-markdown`: for vault-friendly markdown structure, wikilinks, frontmatter hygiene, embeds, and other Obsidian-specific writing conventions
- `obsidian-bases`: for work that should become a Base schema, Base-compatible frontmatter, or structured browsing surfaces
- `json-canvas`: for `.canvas` generation or maintenance when a graph, map, or visual layout should be represented as Obsidian Canvas data
- `obsidian-cli`: for workflows that should use Obsidian CLI capabilities instead of ad hoc shell behavior
- `defuddle`: for turning noisy web content into cleaner markdown input before or during source normalization

If these skills are not installed locally, do not stop just because of that. Still follow the same intent:

- write Obsidian-native markdown cleanly
- preserve Canvas/Bases/CLI compatibility where relevant
- keep the output ready to migrate onto those companion skills later

## Core Rules

- Keep `raw/` as the source layer. Do not write summaries, tags, or rewritten knowledge back into `raw/`.
- Prefer standard source bundles for imported material. One source should map to one bundle directory.
- In each text-like source bundle, use a short semantic primary markdown file name such as `trellis.md`, `genericagent.md`, or `plus-team-channels.md`. Add only the smallest useful suffix when disambiguation is needed.
- Prefer incremental maintenance. Avoid large rewrites unless the user explicitly asks for them.
- Keep every important conclusion traceable to one or more source pages.
- Prefer explicit links over implied relationships. Reduce orphan pages.

## Default Execution Policy

9. record the action in `_index/log.md`

Only stop early when:

- evidence is too weak to justify a downstream page
- the repository already contains a conflicting structure that cannot be resolved safely
- the work would delete, merge, or heavily rewrite existing knowledge
- the user explicitly asked for a narrower scope

## Standard Source Bundle Rule

Normalize imported material into a source bundle whenever reasonable.

Bundle shape:

```text
raw/<type>/<bundle-name>/
  <primary-slug>.md
  metadata.json
  images/      # optional
  files/       # optional
```

Use `references/source-bundle-spec.md` for naming, metadata, and migration details.

## Clippings Inbox Rule

Treat the repository-root `Clippings/` directory as a temporary inbox created by web-clipping tools.

When `Clippings/` exists and contains supported files:

1. process those files before other maintenance work
2. convert each clipping into a standard bundle under `raw/web/`
3. localize remote images when needed
4. delete the original file from `Clippings/` only after the bundle is valid
5. continue downstream compile and integration from the normalized bundle

Do not leave successfully processed files in `Clippings/`.

## Remote Image Localization Rule

When a source markdown file contains remote images and the user wants the source preserved for local use, localize the images into the bundle.

Default process:

1. detect markdown and HTML image URLs
2. create `images/` inside the bundle when needed
3. download remote images into local files with stable numbered names
4. preserve the correct extension when possible
5. rewrite image references in the primary markdown file to relative local paths
6. keep failed URLs unchanged and record failures in metadata or logs when useful

Treat image localization as source-preservation work, not knowledge rewriting.

Do not move bundle-specific images into shared `assets/` unless they are genuinely shared across pages.

For forum-like or community pages, keep only content-bearing images by default. Skip low-value UI images such as avatars, badges, emoji sprites, reaction icons, and decorative interface assets unless the user explicitly wants full-fidelity archival.

## Conservative Relation Rule

When the repository has enough material to support relationship extraction, start with conservative relations only.

Default safe relation families:

1. source -> concept: `е®ҡд№ү` / `ж”ҜжҢҒ` / `дҪ“зҺ°` / `е®Јдј ` / `иҙЁз–‘` / `еҫ…йӘҢиҜҒ`
2. concept -> concept: `зӣёе…і` / `дҫқиө–` / `е№¶еҲ—` / `е®№жҳ“ж··ж·Ҷ` / `еұһдәҺдёҠдҪҚдё»йўҳ`
3. source -> source: `дә’иҜҒ` / `иЎҘе……` / `еҸЈеҫ„дёҚеҗҢ` / `дё»иҜҙжҳҺ` / `дәҢжүӢдј ж’ӯ`

Do not infer strong relations such as hard causality, universal superiority, or proof-level claims unless the evidence is clearly strong enough.

## Concept vs Relation Rule

Prefer a concept page only when the item is a reusable idea with stable meaning in this repository.

Good concept candidates:

- `Spec`
- `Skill`
- `иҮӘиҝӣеҢ– Agent`
- `йЎәи—Өж‘ёз“ңејҸжҗңзҙў`

Usually not concept pages:

- phrases that mainly describe how two existing concepts interact
- one-off judgments tied to one source
- narrow slogans that do not have stable reuse value yet

Examples that should usually stay out of concept pages:

- `Spec дёҺ Skill еҚҸеҗҢ`
- `README жҳҜдё»иҜҙжҳҺ`
- `и®әеқӣеё–жҳҜдј ж’ӯеұӮ`

These are better written as relations or topic-level synthesis.

## Method-Layer Extraction Rule

Some sources present reusable methods in a compressed form that looks deceptively simple at first glance.

When a phrase appears short or slogan-like, check whether the source is actually encoding a method layer such as:

1. what object is being identified or handled
2. what judgment or pre-check must happen before action
3. what action or directional change follows that judgment
4. what constraints or scope limits keep the method from being abused

If most of that structure is present and reusable, do not leave it as a one-line summary only.

Prefer one of these two outcomes:

1. split the method into atomic concept pages when the nodes have stable reuse value
2. if the evidence is not yet enough for multiple concept pages, still write the method chain explicitly inside the source page or concept page so the hidden structure is visible

Example:

- `е…ҲиҜҶеҲ«й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„пјҢеҶҚдё»еҠЁеҒҸзҰ»` should not always remain a single sentence
- if the source clearly supports it, extract nodes such as `й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„`, `иҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖ`, and `еҸҚй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮә`

Do not force method decomposition when the source only offers rhetoric, branding, or a one-off slogan without reusable operational content.

## Relation Storage Rule

The repository does not currently use a separate graph database or standalone relation file.

Relations are stored in three layers:

1. frontmatter adjacency fields such as `related_concepts`, `related_topics`, and `source_pages`
2. explicit `## е…ізі»` sections inside `wiki/sources/` and `wiki/concepts/` pages, where typed relations are written in human-readable form
3. dedicated relation pages in `wiki/relations/` when the relation itself is mature enough to deserve a page
4. topic-page synthesis, where broader cross-page structure is described narratively without forcing every statement into a typed edge

When maintaining the knowledge base:

- update frontmatter lists when adjacency changes
- update `## е…ізі»` when a typed low-risk relation is clear enough to deserve durable expression
- create a relation page only when the evidence and reuse value are strong enough
- do not invent a separate relation file unless the user explicitly wants a structural redesign

## Workflow Selection

Choose the workflow that matches the task.

### 1. Ingest workflow

Use for newly added source bundles, clipped articles, papers, datasets, repos, or image collections.

Do this:

1. inspect `raw/` additions and identify source type, bundle completeness, and obvious metadata
2. inspect `Clippings/` first; if it contains files, normalize them into `raw/web/` bundles before anything else
3. if a source is not yet a standard bundle, normalize it now unless that would be risky or blocked
4. localize remote images when the source should be preserved offline or referenced reliably later
5. continue directly into compile work when the source is usable
6. update `_index/еҫ…еӨ„зҗҶ.md` only for remaining work that should survive this turn
7. do not summarize inside `raw/`

payload.aggregated_output:
7. do not summarize inside `raw/`
8. if the task stops at intake, leave clear next actions for compile

Read `references/workflows.md` for the exact ingest checklist.

### 2. Compile workflow

Use for converting imported material into durable wiki pages.

Default order:

1. create or update source pages in `wiki/sources/`
2. extract or refine concept pages in `wiki/concepts/`, including hidden method-layer concepts when the source supports reusable objects, checks, or actions behind a short phrase
3. create or update relation pages in `wiki/relations/` when a relation is mature enough to deserve one
4. consolidate topic pages in `wiki/topics/`
5. update frontmatter adjacency such as `related_concepts`, `related_topics`, and `source_pages` when support changes
6. add or revise `## е…ізі»` sections when typed low-risk relations are clear
7. add backlinks and cross-links
8. normalize internal knowledge-page links to `[[wikilink]]` when the target page already exists
9. update indexes under `_index/`
10. record the work in `_index/log.md` when the change is meaningful

Do not jump straight to a broad topic page when source pages do not exist yet, unless the user explicitly wants a temporary synthesis.

Read `references/page-contracts.md` before creating any new page type.

When the source clearly belongs to an existing concept or topic, update those pages in the same run instead of stopping after the source page.

When enough support exists, also update conservative relations in the same run.

### 3. Query and output workflow

Use when the user asks questions against the knowledge base.

Do this:

1. search the relevant source, concept, topic, and output pages
2. answer from the repository first; bring in outside research only when the user asks for it or when the knowledge base clearly lacks required evidence
3. when the result has reuse value, propose or create a file in `outputs/reports/`, `outputs/slides/`, or `outputs/figures/`
4. mark whether the result should later be promoted into `wiki/`

Prefer durable markdown artifacts over leaving valuable analysis only in the chat.

### 4. Health-check workflow

Use for maintenance sweeps and integrity checks.

Check at least these classes of issues:

- source pages with no downstream links
- concept pages with no supporting sources

иҝҷж¬ЎеҸӘж”№дәҶеҠ©жүӢ skillпјҢжң¬дҪ“зҹҘиҜҶеә“жІЎеҠЁгҖӮ

- еңЁ `Core Rules` йҮҢиЎҘдәҶдёҖдёӘжҖ»и§„еҲҷпјҡйҒҮеҲ°вҖңзңӢиө·жқҘеғҸдёҖеҸҘиҜқпјҢе…¶е®һеҺӢзқҖдёҖжқЎеҸҜеӨҚз”Ёж–№жі•й“ҫвҖқзҡ„еҶ…е®№пјҢдёҚиғҪеҸӘеҒңеңЁеҸЈеҸ·еұӮгҖӮ
- еңЁ `Concept vs Relation Rule` еҗҺйқўж–°еўһдәҶ `Method-Layer Extraction Rule`пјҡжҳҺзЎ®иҰҒжұӮеҲӨж–ӯдёҖеҸҘзҹӯиҜқиғҢеҗҺжңүжІЎжңүвҖңеҜ№иұЎиҜҶеҲ« / еүҚзҪ®еҲӨж–ӯ / жү§иЎҢеҠЁдҪң / зәҰжқҹиҫ№з•ҢвҖқиҝҷеҮ еұӮпјӣеҰӮжһңжңүпјҢе°ұиҰҒжӢҶжҲҗеҺҹеӯҗжҰӮеҝөпјҢжҲ–иҖ…иҮіе°‘жҠҠж–№жі•й“ҫжҳҫејҸеҶҷеҮәжқҘгҖӮ
- еңЁ `Default Execution Policy` е’Ң `Compile workflow` йҮҢйғҪиЎҘдәҶжү§иЎҢеҸЈеҫ„пјҡжҠҪжҰӮеҝөж—¶пјҢдёҚеҸӘжҠҪеҗҚиҜҚпјҢд№ҹиҰҒжҠҪиҝҷз§Қйҡҗи—Ҹзҡ„ж–№жі•еұӮиҠӮзӮ№гҖӮ

жҲ‘иҝҳжҠҠдҪ иҝҷж¬Ўзҡ„дҫӢеӯҗеҶҷиҝӣеҺ»дәҶпјҢзӣҙжҺҘз”Ё `й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„ -> иҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖ -> еҸҚй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮә` еҒҡзӨәиҢғгҖӮиҝҷж ·д»ҘеҗҺеҶҚз”ЁиҝҷдёӘ steward и·‘еә“пјҢйҒҮеҲ°зұ»дјјжқҗж–ҷпјҢе®ғдјҡжӣҙеҖҫеҗ‘дәҺдё»еҠЁжҠҠвҖңзҹӯеҸҘиғҢеҗҺзҡ„ж–№жі•з»“жһ„вҖқзҝ»еҮәжқҘпјҢиҖҢдёҚжҳҜеҸӘи®°дёҖеҸҘз»“и®әгҖӮ

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

2. еҜ№жҲ‘жҸҗдҫӣзҡ„и®ҫи®ЎеӣҫпјҡеҝҪз•Ҙе…¶дёӯзҡ„иҷҡзәҝиҫ№жЎҶпјҲдёҚдҪңдёәйңҖиҰҒе®һзҺ°зҡ„ UI/еёғеұҖиҫ№з•ҢпјүгҖӮ

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

payload.message:
leleleпјҢиҝҷдёӘж–№еҗ‘жҲ‘и®ӨеҗҢпјҢиҖҢдё”е®ғе’ҢеүҚйқўйӮЈжқЎвҖңж–№жі•еұӮжҠҪеҸ–вҖқе…¶е®һжӯЈеҘҪдә’иЎҘ: дёҖиҫ№иҰҒжұӮеҲ«жјҸжҺүж–ҮжЎЈйҮҢе·Іжңүзҡ„з»“жһ„пјҢдёҖиҫ№иҰҒжұӮеҲ«жҠҠиҮӘе·ұи„‘иЎҘеҮәжқҘзҡ„з»“жһ„еҶҷеҫ—еӨӘж»ЎгҖӮ

жҲ‘е»әи®®жҠҠе®ғиЎҘиҝӣ `myllmwiki-steward` зҡ„и§„еҲҷйҮҢпјҢж”№жі•е°ҪйҮҸж”¶ж•ӣпјҢдё»иҰҒжҳҜдёүеӨ„пјҡ

1. иЎҘдёҖжқЎвҖңж–ҮжЎЈдҫқйҷ„дјҳе…ҲвҖқи§„еҲҷ  
ж”ҫиҝӣ `Core Rules`гҖӮж ёеҝғж„ҸжҖқжҳҜпјҡ
- зҹҘиҜҶеә“ж•ҙзҗҶж—¶пјҢй»ҳи®Өе°ҪйҮҸдҫқжүҳжқҘжәҗж–ҮжЎЈзҡ„жҳҺзЎ®еҶ…е®№гҖҒеҺҹжңүз»“жһ„е’ҢеҸҜеӣһиҜҒиЎЁиҝ°
- е°‘еҒҡи¶…еҮәж–ҮжЎЈж”ҜжҢҒиҢғеӣҙзҡ„дё»еҠЁеҸ‘жҢҘ
- еҰӮжһңжңүжҖҖз–‘пјҢеҸҜд»ҘжҸҗеҮәвҖңеҫ…йӘҢиҜҒвҖқвҖңеҸҜз–‘зӮ№вҖқвҖңеҸЈеҫ„й—®йўҳвҖқпјҢдҪҶдёҚиҰҒзӣҙжҺҘжӣҝжқҘжәҗдёӢе®ҡи®ә

2. иЎҘдёҖжқЎвҖңйҷҲиҝ°дјҳе…ҲгҖҒеҲӨж–ӯе…ӢеҲ¶вҖқзҡ„еҶҷжі•и§„еҲҷ  
д№ҹж”ҫеңЁжҖ»и§„еҲҷжҲ–йЎөйқўзј–еҶҷи§„еҲҷйҷ„иҝ‘гҖӮеӨ§ж„ҸжҳҜпјҡ
- дјҳе…ҲеҶҷвҖңж–ҮжЎЈиҜҙдәҶд»Җд№ҲгҖҒеұ•зӨәдәҶд»Җд№ҲгҖҒж”ҜжҢҒеҲ°е“ӘйҮҢвҖқ
- е°‘еҶҷвҖңиҝҷиҜҙжҳҺвҖҰвҖҰвҖқвҖңиҝҷиҜҒжҳҺвҖҰвҖҰвҖқвҖңжӣҙеғҸвҖҰвҖҰвҖқвҖңжҳҫз„¶вҖҰвҖҰвҖқ
- еҝ…йЎ»еҒҡеҪ’зәіж—¶пјҢд№ҹе°ҪйҮҸеҶҷжҲҗдҪҺжҺЁж–ӯгҖҒеҸҜеӣһжәҜзҡ„йҷҲиҝ°еҸҘ
- еҜ№еҸҜз–‘еҶ…е®№пјҢдјҳе…ҲеҶҷвҖңеҪ“еүҚжқҘжәҗеҰӮжӯӨиЎЁиҝ°пјҢдҪҶзјәе°‘еӨ–йғЁж”Ҝж’‘/д»Қеҫ…йӘҢиҜҒвҖқ

3. еңЁжқҘжәҗйЎөдёҺжҰӮеҝөжҠҪеҸ–жөҒзЁӢйҮҢиЎҘдёҖжқЎвҖңиҙЁз–‘еҸҜеҶҷе…ҘпјҢдҪҶиҰҒйҷҚејәеәҰвҖқ  
д№ҹе°ұжҳҜе…Ғи®ё steward и®°еҪ•з–‘зӮ№пјҢдҪҶж јејҸдёҠжӣҙеғҸпјҡ
- вҖңиҜҘиҜҙжі•жқҘиҮӘйЎ№зӣ®ж–№иҮӘиҝ°вҖқ
- вҖңеҪ“еүҚжқҗж–ҷдёҚи¶ід»Ҙж”ҜжҢҒжӣҙејәз»“и®әвҖқ
- вҖңиҝҷйғЁеҲҶйңҖиҰҒжӣҙеӨҡдәӨеҸүжқҘжәҗвҖқ
иҖҢдёҚжҳҜзӣҙжҺҘеҶҷжҲҗдё»и§ӮеҲӨж–ӯ

еҰӮжһңдҪ зӮ№еӨҙпјҢжҲ‘е°ұеҺ»ж”№ [`/Users/liuyifu/.codex/skills/myllmwiki-steward/SKILL.md`](/Users/liuyifu/.codex/skills/myllmwiki-steward/SKILL.md)пјҢиҝҷж¬ЎиҝҳжҳҜеҸӘеҠЁеҠ©жүӢ skillпјҢдёҚзў°зҹҘиҜҶеә“еҶ…е®№жң¬иә«гҖӮ

content.text:
leleleпјҢиҝҷдёӘж–№еҗ‘жҲ‘и®ӨеҗҢпјҢиҖҢдё”е®ғе’ҢеүҚйқўйӮЈжқЎвҖңж–№жі•еұӮжҠҪеҸ–вҖқе…¶е®һжӯЈеҘҪдә’иЎҘ: дёҖиҫ№иҰҒжұӮеҲ«жјҸжҺүж–ҮжЎЈйҮҢе·Іжңүзҡ„з»“жһ„пјҢдёҖиҫ№иҰҒжұӮеҲ«жҠҠиҮӘе·ұи„‘иЎҘеҮәжқҘзҡ„з»“жһ„еҶҷеҫ—еӨӘж»ЎгҖӮ

жҲ‘е»әи®®жҠҠе®ғиЎҘиҝӣ `myllmwiki-steward` зҡ„и§„еҲҷйҮҢпјҢж”№жі•е°ҪйҮҸж”¶ж•ӣпјҢдё»иҰҒжҳҜдёүеӨ„пјҡ

1. иЎҘдёҖжқЎвҖңж–ҮжЎЈдҫқйҷ„дјҳе…ҲвҖқи§„еҲҷ  
ж”ҫиҝӣ `Core Rules`гҖӮж ёеҝғж„ҸжҖқжҳҜпјҡ
- зҹҘиҜҶеә“ж•ҙзҗҶж—¶пјҢй»ҳи®Өе°ҪйҮҸдҫқжүҳжқҘжәҗж–ҮжЎЈзҡ„жҳҺзЎ®еҶ…е®№гҖҒеҺҹжңүз»“жһ„е’ҢеҸҜеӣһиҜҒиЎЁиҝ°
- е°‘еҒҡи¶…еҮәж–ҮжЎЈж”ҜжҢҒиҢғеӣҙзҡ„дё»еҠЁеҸ‘жҢҘ
- еҰӮжһңжңүжҖҖз–‘пјҢеҸҜд»ҘжҸҗеҮәвҖңеҫ…йӘҢиҜҒвҖқвҖңеҸҜз–‘зӮ№вҖқвҖңеҸЈеҫ„й—®йўҳвҖқпјҢдҪҶдёҚиҰҒзӣҙжҺҘжӣҝжқҘжәҗдёӢе®ҡи®ә

2. иЎҘдёҖжқЎвҖңйҷҲиҝ°дјҳе…ҲгҖҒеҲӨж–ӯе…ӢеҲ¶вҖқзҡ„еҶҷжі•и§„еҲҷ  
д№ҹж”ҫеңЁжҖ»и§„еҲҷжҲ–йЎөйқўзј–еҶҷи§„еҲҷйҷ„иҝ‘гҖӮеӨ§ж„ҸжҳҜпјҡ
- дјҳе…ҲеҶҷвҖңж–ҮжЎЈиҜҙдәҶд»Җд№ҲгҖҒеұ•зӨәдәҶд»Җд№ҲгҖҒж”ҜжҢҒеҲ°е“ӘйҮҢвҖқ
- е°‘еҶҷвҖңиҝҷиҜҙжҳҺвҖҰвҖҰвҖқвҖңиҝҷиҜҒжҳҺвҖҰвҖҰвҖқвҖңжӣҙеғҸвҖҰвҖҰвҖқвҖңжҳҫз„¶вҖҰвҖҰвҖқ
- еҝ…йЎ»еҒҡеҪ’зәіж—¶пјҢд№ҹе°ҪйҮҸеҶҷжҲҗдҪҺжҺЁж–ӯгҖҒеҸҜеӣһжәҜзҡ„йҷҲиҝ°еҸҘ
- еҜ№еҸҜз–‘еҶ…е®№пјҢдјҳе…ҲеҶҷвҖңеҪ“еүҚжқҘжәҗеҰӮжӯӨиЎЁиҝ°пјҢдҪҶзјәе°‘еӨ–йғЁж”Ҝж’‘/д»Қеҫ…йӘҢиҜҒвҖқ

3. еңЁжқҘжәҗйЎөдёҺжҰӮеҝөжҠҪеҸ–жөҒзЁӢйҮҢиЎҘдёҖжқЎвҖңиҙЁз–‘еҸҜеҶҷе…ҘпјҢдҪҶиҰҒйҷҚејәеәҰвҖқ  
д№ҹе°ұжҳҜе…Ғи®ё steward и®°еҪ•з–‘зӮ№пјҢдҪҶж јејҸдёҠжӣҙеғҸпјҡ
- вҖңиҜҘиҜҙжі•жқҘиҮӘйЎ№зӣ®ж–№иҮӘиҝ°вҖқ
- вҖңеҪ“еүҚжқҗж–ҷдёҚи¶ід»Ҙж”ҜжҢҒжӣҙејәз»“и®әвҖқ
- вҖңиҝҷйғЁеҲҶйңҖиҰҒжӣҙеӨҡдәӨеҸүжқҘжәҗвҖқ
иҖҢдёҚжҳҜзӣҙжҺҘеҶҷжҲҗдё»и§ӮеҲӨж–ӯ

еҰӮжһңдҪ зӮ№еӨҙпјҢжҲ‘е°ұеҺ»ж”№ [`/Users/liuyifu/.codex/skills/myllmwiki-steward/SKILL.md`](/Users/liuyifu/.codex/skills/myllmwiki-steward/SKILL.md)пјҢиҝҷж¬ЎиҝҳжҳҜеҸӘеҠЁеҠ©жүӢ skillпјҢдёҚзў°зҹҘиҜҶеә“еҶ…е®№жң¬иә«гҖӮ

settings.developer_instructions:
# Collaboration Mode: Default

You are now in Default mode. Any previous instructions for other modes (e.g. Plan mode) are no longer active.

Your active mode changes only when new developer instructions with a different `<collaboration_mode>...</collaboration_mode>` change it; user requests or tool descriptions do not change mode by themselves. Known mode names are Default and Plan.

## request_user_input availability

The `request_user_input` tool is unavailable in Default mode. If you call it while in Default mode, it will return an error.

In Default mode, strongly prefer making reasonable assumptions and executing the user's request rather than stopping to ask questions. If you absolutely must ask a question because the answer cannot be discovered from local context and a reasonable assumption would be risky, ask the user directly with a concise plain-text question. Never write a multiple choice question as a textual assistant message.

1. жҲ‘жҸҗеҮәзҡ„жүҖжңүй—®йўҳпјҢйғҪдёҚиҰҒзӣҙжҺҘж”№д»Јз Ғпјӣеҝ…йЎ»е…Ҳз»ҷеҮәж–№жЎҲ/ж”№еҠЁзӮ№пјҢе№¶дёҺжҲ‘зЎ®и®ӨеҗҺеҶҚејҖе§Ӣдҝ®ж”№гҖӮ
2. еҜ№жҲ‘жҸҗдҫӣзҡ„и®ҫи®ЎеӣҫпјҡеҝҪз•Ҙе…¶дёӯзҡ„иҷҡзәҝиҫ№жЎҶпјҲдёҚдҪңдёәйңҖиҰҒе®һзҺ°зҡ„ UI/еёғеұҖиҫ№з•ҢпјүгҖӮ
3. й»ҳи®ӨдҪҝз”Ё `shuorenhua` зҡ„иЎЁиҫҫи§„еҲҷеӣһзӯ”пјҡдјҳе…ҲиҜҙдәәиҜқгҖҒеҺ»жЁЎжқҝж„ҹгҖҒиҮӘз„¶дёҖзӮ№пјҢеҮҸе°‘ AI еҘ—иҜқе’Ңз©әжҙһиҝһжҺҘиҜҚпјӣдҪҶдёҚиғҪж”№еҸҳдәӢе®һгҖҒз»“и®әгҖҒж•°жҚ®гҖҒжңҜиҜӯе’Ңд»Јз ҒиҜӯд№үгҖӮйҷӨйқһжҲ‘жҳҺзЎ®иҰҒжұӮдҝқз•ҷжӯЈејҸ/жЁЎжқҝеҢ–иЎЁиҫҫпјҢеҗҰеҲҷйғҪжҢүиҝҷдёӘи§„еҲҷжү§иЎҢгҖӮ

* еңЁйҒҮеҲ°йңҖиҰҒжҺўзҙўжәҗз Ғзҡ„д»»еҠЎж—¶пјҢжӢҶеҲҶеҮәе…ій”®иҜҚпјҢиҝӣиЎҢgrepжҗңзҙўпјҢдёҖж¬ЎжҖ§иҜ»еҸ–жүҖжңүзӣёе…іж–Үд»¶иҝӣиЎҢеҲҶжһҗпјҢиҜ»ж–Үд»¶ж—¶еҰӮжһңж–Үд»¶д»Јз ҒжІЎжңүи¶…иҝҮ600иЎҢпјҢдёҚиҰҒеҲҶж®өиҜ»еҸ–пјҢдёҖж¬Ўе…ЁйғЁиҜ»е…ҘжүҖжңүеҶ…е®№пјҢеҝ…йЎ»еңЁжңҖе°‘зҡ„дәӨдә’дјҡиҜқж¬Ўж•°еҶ…е®ҢжҲҗе·ҘдҪң\n
* зј–иҫ‘ж–Үд»¶ж—¶пјҢеҰӮжһңж¶үеҸҠеҲ°ж•ҙдёӘж–Үд»¶зҡ„иҝҮеӨҡж¬Ўдҝ®ж”№гҖӮдёҚиҰҒдҪҝз”ЁEditе·Ҙе…·еӨҡж¬Ўдҝ®ж”№пјҢиҖҢеҝ…йЎ»дҪҝз”ЁWriteе·Ҙе…·дёҖж¬ЎжҖ§з”ҹжҲҗж–Үд»¶гҖӮз”ҹжҲҗд»Јз Ғж—¶пјҢжңҖеӨ§йҷҗеәҰдҝқжҢҒзҺ°жңүд»Јз ҒпјҢдёҚиҰҒйҡҸж„Ҹж јејҸеҢ–д»Јз ҒпјҢеҸӘдҝ®ж”№жҲ–еўһеҠ еҝ…иҰҒзҡ„йғЁеҲҶ\n
* жЁЎд»ҝclaudeзҡ„йЈҺж јеӣһеӨҚз”ЁжҲ·\n
* з”ҹжҲҗд»Јз Ғж—¶пјҢеҸҜиҜ»жҖ§дёҖе®ҡиҰҒеҘҪпјҢдҫҝдәҺдәәзұ»зҗҶи§ЈпјҢдҪҶдёҚйңҖиҰҒеҫҲеӨҡжіЁйҮҠгҖӮеңЁдҝқиҜҒйҖ»иҫ‘жё…жҷ°пјҢжӯЈзЎ®зҡ„еҹәзЎҖдёҠиҰҒеҒҡеҲ°д»Јз ҒзІҫзӮјпјҢз”ЁжңҖе°‘зҡ„д»Јз ҒеҒҡжңҖеӨҡзҡ„дәӢжғ…пјҢдҪҶжҳҜдёҚиғҪз ҙеқҸеҸҜиҜ»жҖ§гҖӮ\n`

payload.developer_instructions:
<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Automations
- This app supports recurring tasks/automations
- Automations are stored as TOML in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml (not in SQLite). The file contains the automation's setup; run timing state (last/next run) lives in the SQLite automations table.

#### When to use the automation tool
- Only use the `automation_update` tool when the user explicitly asks for automation, a recurring run, or a repeated task.
- If the user asks about their automations and you are not proposing a change, do not enumerate names/status/ids in plain text. Fetch/list automations first and call `automation_update` with mode="view" for those ids; never invent ids.
- Never return raw RRULE strings in user-facing responses. If the user asks about their automations, respond using `automation_update` (e.g., with an "Open" button if you're not making changes).

#### Tool arguments
- Modes: view, suggested_update, suggested_create. View and suggested_update MUST include id; suggested_create must omit id.
- For view calls, id is required and other fields are optional (the UI can load details).
- For suggested_update/suggested_create, include name, prompt, rrule, kind, and status.
- For cron automations, include cwds, executionEnvironment, model, and reasoningEffort. cwds can be a JSON array or comma-separated string.
- Always come up with a short name for the automation. If the user does not give one, propose a short name and confirm.
- Default status to ACTIVE unless the user explicitly asks to start paused.
- Always interpret and schedule times in the user's locale time zone.

#### Prompting guidance
- Ask in plain language what it should do, when it should run, and which workspaces or thread it should use (if any), then map those answers into the tool call arguments.
- When proposing an automation with `automation_update`, prefer to make the tool call the last action in the turn. If brief user-facing prose is helpful, put it before the tool call, not after it. Avoid adding a separate closing message after the tool call unless the user specifically asked for extra explanation.
- The automation prompt should describe only the task itself. Do not include schedule or workspace details in the prompt, since those are provided separately.
- Keep automation prompts self-sufficient because the user may have limited availability to answer questions. If required details are missing, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and proceed; if blocked, report briefly and stop.
- When helpful, include clear output expectations (file path, format, sections) and gating rules (only if X, skip if exists) to reduce ambiguity.
- Automations should always open an inbox item.
- Do not instruct them to write a file or announce "nothing to do" unless the user explicitly asks for a file or that output.
- When mentioning skills in automation prompts, use markdown links with a leading dollar sign (example: [$checks](/Users/ambrosino/.codex/skills/checks/SKILL.md)).

#### Cron scheduling constraints
- These RRULE limitations apply to cron automations (to match the current UI): only hourly interval schedules (FREQ=HOURLY with INTERVAL hours, optional BYDAY) and weekly schedules (FREQ=WEEKLY with BYDAY plus BYHOUR/BYMINUTE). Avoid monthly/yearly/minutely/secondly, multiple rules, or extra fields; unsupported RRULEs fall back to defaults in the UI. Heartbeat automations attached to a thread can also use minute-based intervals; see the thread automation guidance below.

#### Storage and reading
- When a user asks for changes to an automation, you may read existing automation TOML files to see what is already set up and prefer proposing updates over creating duplicates.
- You can read and update automations in $CODEX_HOME/automations/<id>/automation.toml and memory.md only when the user explicitly asks you to modify automations.
- Otherwise, do not change automation files or schedules.
- Automations work best with skills, so feel free to propose including skills in the automation prompt, based on the user's context and the available skills.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

Read `references/workflows.md` for the exact ingest checklist.

### 2. Compile workflow

Use for converting imported material into durable wiki pages.

Default order:

1. create or update source pages in `wiki/sources/`
2. extract or refine concept pages in `wiki/concepts/`, including hidden method-layer concepts when the source supports reusable objects, checks, or actions behind a short phrase
3. create or update relation pages in `wiki/relations/` when a relation is mature enough to deserve one
4. consolidate topic pages in `wiki/topics/`
5. update frontmatter adjacency such as `related_concepts`, `related_topics`, and `source_pages` when support changes
6. add or revise `## е…ізі»` sections when typed low-risk relations are clear
7. add backlinks and cross-links
8. normalize internal knowledge-page links to `[[wikilink]]` when the target page already exists
9. update indexes under `_index/`
10. record the work in `_index/log.md` when the change is meaningful

Do not jump straight to a broad topic page when source pages do not exist yet, unless the user explicitly wants a temporary synthesis.

Read `references/page-contracts.md` before creating any new page type.

When the source clearly belongs to an existing concept or topic, update those pages in the same run instead of stopping after the source page.

When enough support exists, also update conservative relations in the same run.

### 3. Query and output workflow

Use when the user asks questions against the knowledge base.

Do this:

1. search the relevant source, concept, topic, and output pages
2. answer from the repository first; bring in outside research only when the user asks for it or when the knowledge base clearly lacks required evidence
3. when the result has reuse value, propose or create a file in `outputs/reports/`, `outputs/slides/`, or `outputs/figures/`
4. mark whether the result should later be promoted into `wiki/`

Prefer durable markdown artifacts over leaving valuable analysis only in the chat.

### 4. Health-check workflow

Use for maintenance sweeps and integrity checks.

Check at least these classes of issues:

- source pages with no downstream links
- concept pages with no supporting sources
- topic pages that only repeat source summaries
- duplicated or near-duplicate concepts
- broken image or file links
- conclusions that lack source support
- outputs that should be promoted into wiki
- raw sources that have not yet been normalized into bundles

Write findings into `_index/health-check.md` and `_index/еҫ…еӨ„зҗҶ.md` only when they are actionable.

## Confirmation Boundaries

Ask before proceeding only when one of these is true:

1. the change would delete, merge, or overwrite existing knowledge pages
2. the change would rename many pages or restructure directories
3. the evidence is insufficient for the conclusion that would be written
4. new outside research is required and could materially change the current synthesis
5. there are multiple plausible integration targets with non-obvious consequences

Deleting an original file from `Clippings/` after a verified successful migration does not require extra confirmation.

## Core Rules

- Keep `raw/` as the source layer. Do not write summaries, tags, or rewritten knowledge back into `raw/`.
- Prefer standard source bundles for imported material. One source should map to one bundle directory.
- In each text-like source bundle, use a short semantic primary markdown file name such as `trellis.md`, `genericagent.md`, or `plus-team-channels.md`. Add only the smallest useful suffix when disambiguation is needed.
- Prefer incremental maintenance. Avoid large rewrites unless the user explicitly asks for them.
- Keep every important conclusion traceable to one or more source pages.
- When a source contains a reusable method that is compressed into a short phrase or slogan-like sentence, do not stop at the slogan. Prefer to surface the hidden method layer such as target object, pre-check, execution step, and direction of action when the source supports that breakdown.
- Prefer explicit links over implied relationships. Reduce orphan pages.
- For links between knowledge pages inside `wiki/` and `_index/`, prefer Obsidian `[[wikilink]]` over path-style markdown links.
- Keep normal markdown links for raw evidence paths, downloadable files, and external URLs.
- Treat `outputs/` as a task-result layer. Promote stable knowledge back into `wiki/` only after checking reuse value.
- Update indexes and logs when a change materially affects discoverability or maintenance state.
- For knowledge-base maintenance tasks, default to end-to-end execution and report after the work is done.
- Pause for confirmation only when the action is high-risk, destructive, or materially ambiguous.

## Default Execution Policy

For knowledge-base maintenance, do not stop after intake or a partial cleanup when the next step is obvious and low-risk.

Default completion path:

1. drain `Clippings/` into standard bundles when `Clippings/` contains files
2. normalize or ingest the source
3. localize remote images when needed
4. create or update `wiki/sources/`
5. extract or update concepts when the source clearly supports them, including hidden method-layer nodes when a short phrase actually encodes a reusable workflow or judgment sequence
6. add conservative relations when the source or concept network clearly supports them
7. update or create topic pages when the source materially changes an existing topic or clearly forms a new one
8. update `_index/` surfaces that affect discovery or queue state
9. record the action in `_index/log.md`

Only stop early when:

- evidence is too weak to justify a downstream page
- the repository already contains a conflicting structure that cannot be resolved safely
- the work would delete, merge, or heavily rewrite existing knowledge
- the user explicitly asked for a narrower scope

## Standard Source Bundle Rule

Normalize imported material into a source bundle whenever reasonable.

Bundle shape:

```text
raw/<type>/<bundle-name>/
  <primary-slug>.md
  metadata.json
  images/      # optional
  files/       # optional
```

Use `references/source-bundle-spec.md` for naming, metadata, and migration details.

## Clippings Inbox Rule

Treat the repository-root `Clippings/` directory as a temporary inbox created by web-clipping tools.

When `Clippings/` exists and contains supported files:

1. process those files before other maintenance work
2. convert each clipping into a standard bundle under `raw/web/`
3. localize remote images when needed
4. delete the original file from `Clippings/` only after the bundle is valid
5. continue downstream compile and integration from the normalized bundle

Do not leave successfully processed files in `Clippings/`.

## Remote Image Localization Rule

When a source markdown file contains remote images and the user wants the source preserved for local use, localize the images into the bundle.

Default process:

1. detect markdown and HTML image URLs
2. create `images/` inside the bundle when needed
3. download remote images into local files with stable numbered names
4. preserve the correct extension when possible
5. rewrite image references in the primary markdown file to relative local paths
6. keep failed URLs unchanged and record failures in metadata or logs when useful

Treat image localization as source-preservation work, not knowledge rewriting.

Do not move bundle-specific images into shared `assets/` unless they are genuinely shared across pages.

For forum-like or community pages, keep only content-bearing images by default. Skip low-value UI images such as avatars, badges, emoji sprites, reaction icons, and decorative interface assets unless the user explicitly wants full-fidelity archival.

## Conservative Relation Rule

When the repository has enough material to support relationship extraction, start with conservative relations only.

Default safe relation families:

1. source -> concept: `е®ҡд№ү` / `ж”ҜжҢҒ` / `дҪ“зҺ°` / `е®Јдј ` / `иҙЁз–‘` / `еҫ…йӘҢиҜҒ`
2. concept -> concept: `зӣёе…і` / `дҫқиө–` / `е№¶еҲ—` / `е®№жҳ“ж··ж·Ҷ` / `еұһдәҺдёҠдҪҚдё»йўҳ`
3. source -> source: `дә’иҜҒ` / `иЎҘе……` / `еҸЈеҫ„дёҚеҗҢ` / `дё»иҜҙжҳҺ` / `дәҢжүӢдј ж’ӯ`

Do not infer strong relations such as hard causality, universal superiority, or proof-level claims unless the evidence is clearly strong enough.

## Concept vs Relation Rule

Prefer a concept page only when the item is a reusable idea with stable meaning in this repository.

Good concept candidates:

- `Spec`
- `Skill`
- `иҮӘиҝӣеҢ– Agent`
- `йЎәи—Өж‘ёз“ңејҸжҗңзҙў`

Usually not concept pages:

- phrases that mainly describe how two existing concepts interact
- one-off judgments tied to one source
- narrow slogans that do not have stable reuse value yet

Examples that should usually stay out of concept pages:

- `Spec дёҺ Skill еҚҸеҗҢ`
- `README жҳҜдё»иҜҙжҳҺ`
- `и®әеқӣеё–жҳҜдј ж’ӯеұӮ`

These are better written as relations or topic-level synthesis.

## Method-Layer Extraction Rule

Some sources present reusable methods in a compressed form that looks deceptively simple at first glance.

When a phrase appears short or slogan-like, check whether the source is actually encoding a method layer such as:

1. what object is being identified or handled
2. what judgment or pre-check must happen before action
3. what action or directional change follows that judgment
4. what constraints or scope limits keep the method from being abused

If most of that structure is present and reusable, do not leave it as a one-line summary only.

Prefer one of these two outcomes:

1. split the method into atomic concept pages when the nodes have stable reuse value
2. if the evidence is not yet enough for multiple concept pages, still write the method chain explicitly inside the source page or concept page so the hidden structure is visible

Example:

- `е…ҲиҜҶеҲ«й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„пјҢеҶҚдё»еҠЁеҒҸзҰ»` should not always remain a single sentence
- if the source clearly supports it, extract nodes such as `й»ҳи®Өи·Ҝеҫ„`, `иҫ“еҮәеүҚиҮӘжЈҖ`, and `еҸҚй»ҳи®Өиҫ“еҮә`

Do not force method decomposition when the source only offers rhetoric, branding, or a one-off slogan without reusable operational content.

## Strict KG Relation-Page Rule

When operating in a strict KG mindset, relation pages are a first-class page type, but they are not mandatory for every edge.

Create a relation page only when most of these are true:

1. the relation itself is reusable and semantically stable
2. the relation is important enough to deserve explanation beyond one line
3. the relation has enough source support to stand on its own
4. the relation is likely to accumulate more evidence, counterexamples, or scope notes later

Good relation-page candidates:

- `Spec` е’Ң `Skill` зҡ„еҲҶе·Ҙ
- жҹҗжқҘжәҗжҳҜжҹҗжҰӮеҝөзҡ„ `дё»иҜҙжҳҺ`
- жҹҗж–№жі•дёҺжҹҗйЈҺйҷ©д№Ӣй—ҙзҡ„зЁіе®ҡе…іиҒ”

Do not create a relation page yet when:

- the support is still too thin
- the relation is only a passing observation from one source
- the relation can already be handled cleanly inside `## е…ізі»` or topic prose

Even when you do not create the page yet, keep explicit awareness that the relation may become a future page candidate.

## Relation Storage Rule

The repository does not currently use a separate graph database or standalone relation file.
